claim: The steels were prepared by induction melting, heated at 1150 °C, hot rolled at initial rolling temperature of 1050 °C to 3 mm thick strips with a total reduction of 85%, then solution treated at 1100 °C for 1 h and water quenched.
evidence_text: The steels were prepared by induction melting, heated at 1150 ⁰C and subsequently rolled at the initial rolling temperature of 1050 ⁰C to strips of 3 mm thick with a total reduction of 85%. Tensile specimens prepared from the strips were solution-treated at 1100 ⁰C for 1 h and quenched in water.

claim: Optical microscopy shows that 3Al, 6Al, and 10Al steels are fully austenitic, with similar grain size of about 80 μm when excluding twin boundaries.
evidence_text: a–c shows that 3Al, 6Al, and 10Al steels were fully austenitic when imaged using an optical microscope... Excluding the twin boundaries, the grain size of 3Al, 6Al, and 10Al steels was similar at 80 μm

claim: The 12Al steel contains 7% ferrite (small white block phase) and austenite grains of 40 μm (excluding twin boundaries) or 16 μm (including twin boundaries).
evidence_text: ferrite phase was observed in 12Al steel, Figure 1d. Excluding the twin boundaries... the grain size of austenite in 12Al steel was significantly smaller ( 40 μm). When the twin boundaries were considered, the grain size of... 12Al steel was 16 μm. The volume fraction of ferrite... measured by image analysis technique was 7%.

claim: TEM reveals weak superlattice spots in 10Al steel and strong superlattice spots in 12Al steel, indicating the presence of (Fe,Mn)3AlCx κ carbides, while only fundamental reflections appear in 3Al and 6Al steels.
evidence_text: TEM observations indicated only fundamental reflections in 3Al and 6Al steels, but weak superlattice spots between the fundamental reflections appeared in 10Al steel (Figure 1e) and strong ones in 12Al steel (Figure 1f), indicating the presence of ( Fe , Mn )3AlCx carbides (ordered κ carbides).

claim: The solid solution hardening coefficient kc was determined to be 17.54 MPa per wt% Al from the yield strength difference between 6Al and 3Al steels, and the base yield strength of pure austenite σ_s0 was calculated as 275.57 MPa.
evidence_text: k_c = (σ(6Al) - σ(3Al)) / (C(6Al) - C(3Al)) = (378 - 321) / (5.84 - 2.59) = 17.54 MPa/wt% Al. Thus, the value of σ_s0 calculated was 275.57 MPa.

claim: The solid solution hardening contribution in 10Al steel is 174 MPa, giving a calculated yield strength of 449.57 MPa; the difference of 20.43 MPa from the measured 470 MPa is attributed to limited precipitates.
evidence_text: the solid solution hardening contribution in 10Al steel was calculated to be 174 MPa and the total value of 449.57 MPa was obtained using Equation 1. Given that the grain size... was similar... the difference between the measured and calculated yield strength of 20.43 MPa, was because of limited precipitates in 10Al steel.

claim: Hall–Petch analysis on 6Al steel with grain sizes from 5.38 to 52 μm (including twin boundaries) gives lattice friction stress σ0 = 250.70 MPa and strengthening coefficient kG = 683.07 MPa·μm^1/2.
evidence_text: the lattice friction stress, σ0, was estimated to be 250.70 MPa and the strengthening coefficient, kG, was 683.07 MPa μm1/2.

claim: In 12Al steel, solid solution hardening by Al (overestimated due to Al consumption) contributes 203.11 MPa, grain refinement adds 61.39 MPa, ferrite (7% fraction) provides approximately 50 MPa, and κ-carbide precipitation hardening contributes 361.93 MPa, summing to an actual yield strength of 952 MPa.
evidence_text: The estimated contribution of Al to solid solution to yield strength in 12Al steel was 203.11 MPa... The increment due to grain refinement was 61.39 MPa... The volume fraction of ferrite... was 7%, and 50 MPa was considered as the approximate contribution to yield strength by ferrite... the increase in yield strength contribution by κ precipitates in 12Al steel was deduced to be 361.93 MPa.

claim: Microhardness of austenite in 6Al steel is 244.4 kgf mm^−2, the matrix in 12Al steel is 399.1 kgf mm^−2, and ferrite in 12Al steel is 332.6 kgf mm^−2.
evidence_text: The microhardness of austenite in 6Al steel was 244.4 kgf mm^−2, whereas that of the matrix in 12Al steel was higher at 399.1 kgf mm^−2. The microhardness of ferrite in 12Al steel was 332.6 kgf mm^−2

claim: κ-carbides can form during quenching when Al content exceeds 10 wt% and carbon content is adequate; the modulation contrast in as-quenched 12Al steel is strain contrast from lattice modulation due to solute concentration fluctuations, and the κ-carbide-induced yield strength increase is 361.93 MPa.
evidence_text: It was pointed out that κ-carbides can form during quenching if Al- content exceeds 10 wt% and the alloy contains adequate C-content... the modulation image is the strain contrast that originates from lattice modulation, and is caused by fluctuation in the concentration of solute atoms... the increase in yield strength of 361.93 MPa is caused by κ carbides in the as-quenched 12Al steel.

claim: 3Al and 6Al steels exhibit obvious strain hardening attributed to TWIP effect, whereas 12Al steel shows no remarkable strain hardening due to high Al content increasing stacking fault energy and promoting glide plane softening from large fraction of carbides.
evidence_text: Both 3Al and 6Al steels exhibited obvious strain hardening, which may be attributed to TWIP (twinning-induced plasticity) effect... With increase in Al concentration, the stacking fault energy increases... no remarkable strain hardening in 12Al steel occurs.

claim: TEM thin foils were prepared by twin-jet electropolishing using 30% nitric acid in methanol at −30 °C, and optical microscopy specimens were etched with a solution of 1 ml HCl, 100 ml methanol, and 4 g picric acid.
evidence_text: Specimens for optical microscopy were prepared via standard metallography procedure and etched with a solution consisting of 1 ml hydrochloric acid, 100 ml methanol, and 4 g picric acid. Thin foils for TEM were prepared by twin-jet electropolishing using 30% nitric acid in methanol at 30 ⁰C.

claim: When the Al concentration increases to a certain amount, a large fraction of carbides form and 'glide plane softening' appears, resulting in no remarkable strain hardening in 12Al steel.
evidence_text: When the Al concentration increases to a certain amount, a large fraction of carbides form and 'glide plane softening'appears.[21] Thus, no remarkable strain hardening in 12Al steel occurs.
